# Reranking

## Reranking {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/reranking/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Reranking {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/reranking/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Reranking {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/reranking/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# The chunks aren't the input {.center}

::: lead
Retrieval hands back three objects. The model reads one string. Something has to build that string.
:::

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <rect class="zone" x="40" y="70" width="920" height="230"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="100" text-anchor="middle">RETRIEVAL'S OUTPUT</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="80" y="130" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="168">Paid Time Off</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="192">Policy v2</text>
    <text class="note" x="100" y="226">score 0.481</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="370" y="130" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="168">Paid Time Off</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="390" y="192">Policy v1</text>
    <text class="note" x="390" y="226">score 0.480</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="660" y="130" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="168">Remote Work</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="192">Guidelines</text>
    <text class="note" x="680" y="226">score 0.068</text>
  </g>
  <text class="cap" x="500" y="335" text-anchor="middle">a title, a score, a wall of text -- three separate objects</text>
</svg>
```

A chat model takes plain text in. Three `Chunk` objects are not plain text yet.

::: notes
This is the seam between the two halves of RAG. Everything up to now was ranking. Everything after is about the single string the model receives.
:::

---

## assemble_prompt() turns them into one string

The retrieved chunks flow into a single card, and each one gains a number as it lands.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="30" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="80" y="66">PTO Policy v2</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="380" y="30" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="400" y="66">PTO Policy v1</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="700" y="30" width="240" height="60" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="720" y="66">Remote Work Guidelines</text>
  </g>

  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 180 92 L 260 195"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 500 92 L 500 195"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 820 92 L 740 195"/>

  <text class="cap big" x="500" y="178" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">assemble_prompt()</text>
  <rect class="card hot" x="120" y="195" width="760" height="170"/>

  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag main" x="150" y="222" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="173" y="243">[1]</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="210" y="243">PTO Policy v2, excerpt</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag feat" x="150" y="264" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="173" y="285">[2]</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="210" y="285">PTO Policy v1, excerpt</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag head" x="150" y="306" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="173" y="327">[3]</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="210" y="327">Remote Work Guidelines, excerpt</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
`assemble_prompt()` is a plain string builder. It walks the chunk list in order and writes each one behind a bracketed number. Grounding holds because this step stays mechanical and inspectable.
:::

---

## Every excerpt is numbered, not named

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 320">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="120" y="60" width="360" height="70" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="145" y="102">Chunk: "PTO Policy v2"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 500 95 L 560 95"/>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="tag main" x="600" y="60" width="60" height="34"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="630" y="83">[1]</text>
    <rect class="card" x="670" y="60" width="260" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="690" y="102">(PTO Policy v2) ...</text>
  </g>
  <text class="cap" x="500" y="200" text-anchor="middle">the number is the excerpt's only name inside the prompt</text>
  <text class="note" x="500" y="240" text-anchor="middle">order of chunks in decides the order of numbers out -- nothing else does</text>
</svg>
```

A chunk's doc title never reaches the model directly. Its `[n]` does.

::: notes
Chunk one becomes `[1]` because it arrives first, nothing more. That fixed order is what lets generation write `[2]` later and the app resolve it back to its source. The mapping was set the moment the prompt was built.
:::

---

## An instruction wraps the excerpts

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="40" y="50" width="280" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="180" y="80" text-anchor="middle">PREAMBLE</text>
    <text class="note" x="180" y="180" text-anchor="middle">the question, plus</text>
    <text class="note" x="180" y="206" text-anchor="middle">"answer using only</text>
    <text class="note" x="180" y="232" text-anchor="middle">the excerpts below"</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="360" y="50" width="280" height="280" style="stroke:var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="80" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--accent)">EXCERPTS</text>
    <rect class="tag main" x="470" y="140" width="60" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="500" y="161">[1]</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="470" y="182" width="60" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="500" y="203">[2]</text>
    <rect class="tag head" x="470" y="224" width="60" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="500" y="245">[3]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="680" y="50" width="280" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="820" y="80" text-anchor="middle">CLOSING RULE</text>
    <text class="note" x="820" y="180" text-anchor="middle">"cite each fact as [n].</text>
    <text class="note" x="820" y="206" text-anchor="middle">If the excerpts don't</text>
    <text class="note" x="820" y="232" text-anchor="middle">cover it, say so."</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Three zones, in this order: an instruction that scopes the model to the excerpts, the numbered excerpts, and a closing rule demanding a citation per claim. That closing rule is what makes cite-or-refuse enforceable. It lives inside the prompt, reaching the model before it writes a word.
:::

---

## What crosses the boundary to the model

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 380">
  <rect class="zone" x="40" y="60" width="420" height="260"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="250" y="90" text-anchor="middle">EVERYTHING RAG KNOWS</text>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="80" y="120" width="150" height="55"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="153">corpus</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="290" y="120" width="150" height="55"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="315" y="153">index</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="80" y="200" width="360" height="55"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="100" y="233">48 chunks</text>
  </g>

  <rect class="zone" x="620" y="60" width="340" height="260" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
  <text class="zonelabel" x="790" y="90" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--amber)">THE MODEL</text>

  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 460 250 L 640 250"/>
    <rect class="card hot" x="660" y="220" width="260" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="680" y="255" style="fill:var(--amber)">the prompt string</text>
  </g>

  <text class="cap" x="500" y="355" text-anchor="middle">corpus, index, and every other chunk never cross -- only this one string does</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Grounding is checkable because the model's whole universe for this answer is the string `assemble_prompt()` returned, rebuilt for each question. Nothing it absorbed in training enters here, because nothing else is in the channel.
:::


# Augmentation: Building the Prompt

## Augmentation: Building the Prompt {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/augmentation/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Augmentation: Building the Prompt {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/augmentation/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Augmentation: Building the Prompt {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/augmentation/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# Cite or Refuse {.center}

::: lead
Retrieval and augmentation hand the model a stack of excerpts. Generation is the
last step. Turn that stack into a sentence a reader can trust, or say the corpus
has no answer.
:::

Every sentence ties back to a source, or the system declines. That is what makes
a RAG answer worth trusting.

::: notes
Same demo query as the retrieval and augmentation slides, now at its two possible
endings. The point to say out loud: there is no third option between a cited
claim and a refusal.
:::

---

## Two ways a question can end

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="255" y="55" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">query: "how many days of PTO do I get"</text>
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="80" width="390" height="260" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="255" y="112" style="fill:var(--feat)">ANSWERED</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="160">Full-time employees accrue 20</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="85" y="188">days of paid time off per year.</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="85" y="212" width="34" height="26"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="102" y="230">[1]</text>
    <text class="note" x="130" y="230">Paid Time Off Policy v2</text>
    <text class="note" x="255" y="316" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">score clears the refusal bar</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="745" y="55" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">query: "what's the weather in Tokyo tomorrow"</text>
    <rect class="card" x="550" y="80" width="390" height="260" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="745" y="112" style="fill:#e06b9c">REFUSED</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="575" y="170">"The corpus doesn't cover this</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="575" y="198">well enough to answer."</text>
    <text class="note" x="745" y="316" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:#e06b9c">score never clears the bar</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="380">Same generator, same gate -- the evidence decides which card you land on.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Nothing in the generator changes between the two endings. The only difference is
what retrieval found. A covered question gets a cited sentence, an uncovered one
gets a decline.
:::

---

## A citation is a pointer, not decoration

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="90">"...accrue 20 days of paid time off per year."</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="710" y="66" width="46" height="30"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="733" y="86">[1]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader feat" d="M 733 96 L 733 210" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <rect class="card" x="330" y="210" width="340" height="130" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="242" style="fill:var(--feat)">RETRIEVED CHUNK</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="352" y="278">Paid Time Off Policy v2</text>
    <text class="note" x="352" y="306">"...accrue 20 days of paid time off..."</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="378" text-anchor="middle">[1] names the chunk the sentence came from, so a reader can check it.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The bracket number resolves to one chunk in the index. A reader can follow it
back to the source and check the claim without trusting the model's wording.
:::

---

## Where the line gets drawn

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" style="marker-end:none;stroke:var(--edge)" d="M 100 220 L 900 220"/>
    <text class="note" x="100" y="252">low similarity</text>
    <text class="note" x="900" y="252" text-anchor="end">high similarity</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader head" d="M 196 190 L 196 250" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="196" y="170" style="fill:var(--amber)">refusal threshold</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle cx="140" cy="220" r="16" style="fill:#3a2230;stroke:#e06b9c;stroke-width:2.5"/>
    <text class="note" x="140" y="290" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:#e06b9c">weather query: 0.00</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node feat" cx="580" cy="220" r="16"/>
    <text class="note" x="580" y="290" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">PTO query: 0.48</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="360">Below the line: refuse. Above it: cite.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The gate is one number, the best similarity score retrieval returned. Set it too
low and the model guesses on thin evidence. Set it too high and it refuses
questions the corpus covers. Tuning that one knob trades the two failure modes
against each other.
:::

---

## No partial credit

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="400" y="50" width="200" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="420" y="92">check top score</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 460 120 L 260 220"/>
    <rect class="card" x="80" y="220" width="320" height="110" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="240" y="252" style="fill:var(--feat)">ABOVE THE BAR</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="105" y="292">write the answer, cite [n]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 540 120 L 740 220"/>
    <rect class="card" x="600" y="220" width="320" height="110" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="760" y="252" style="fill:#e06b9c">BELOW THE BAR</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="625" y="292">decline</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="378" text-anchor="middle">An answer is either grounded, or it doesn't happen -- there is no middle.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A plain language model never has this branch. It always produces text. Cite or
refuse gives the system a way to say "I don't know" instead of a fluent guess.
:::

---

## Grading the gate itself

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 470">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="430" y="90" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">system answered</text>
    <text class="note" x="710" y="90" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">system refused</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="168" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">has an</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="192" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="308" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">has no</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="332" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--muted)">answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="300" y="110" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="430" y="176" style="fill:var(--feat)">correct answer</text>
    <rect class="card" x="580" y="250" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="710" y="316" style="fill:var(--feat)">correct refusal</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="580" y="110" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:var(--amber)"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="710" y="176" style="fill:var(--amber)">over-refusal</text>
    <rect class="card" x="300" y="250" width="260" height="120" style="stroke:#e06b9c;stroke-width:3"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="430" y="304" style="fill:#e06b9c">FALSE ANSWER</text>
    <text class="note" x="430" y="332" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:#e06b9c">answered with no source to stand on</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="410" text-anchor="middle">false-answer rate: how often it answers a no-answer question</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="434" text-anchor="middle">refusal precision: of its refusals, how many truly had no answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="458" text-anchor="middle">refusal recall: of the no-answer questions, how many it caught</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Treat answer-or-refuse as its own label, separate from whether the answer text
reads well. The bottom-left cell is the one that costs trust: a confident answer
where the corpus has nothing. Precision and recall on refusals show which way
the threshold is skewed.
:::

---

## A citation can be wrong even when the answer is right

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cardtext" x="500" y="70" text-anchor="middle">"PTO accrues monthly [1] and rolls over each year [2]."</text>
    <rect class="tag feat" x="330" y="86" width="40" height="28"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="350" y="106">[1]</text>
    <rect class="tag head" x="600" y="86" width="40" height="28"/>
    <text class="tagtext" x="620" y="106">[2]</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader feat" d="M 350 118 L 250 220" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <rect class="card" x="120" y="222" width="260" height="90" style="stroke:var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="140" y="258">"...accrues monthly..."</text>
    <text class="note" x="140" y="286" style="fill:var(--feat)">supports the claim</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="leader head" d="M 620 118 L 720 220" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <rect class="card" x="600" y="222" width="260" height="90" style="stroke:#e06b9c;stroke-width:3"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="620" y="258">"...5 unused days max..."</text>
    <text class="note" x="620" y="286" style="fill:#e06b9c">says nothing about rollover</text>
  </g>
  <text class="cap big" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">[2] points at a real chunk. It just isn't the one that backs this sentence.</text>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Grading generation isn't just "did it cite something." Check each citation
against the claim it's attached to, check every load-bearing claim has one,
and check whether a better-supporting chunk existed but got skipped. A
correct-sounding answer with a mismatched citation still fails.
:::


# Generation: Cite or Refuse

## Generation: Cite or Refuse {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/generation/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Generation: Cite or Refuse {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/generation/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

## Generation: Cite or Refuse {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/generation/step-03.png){fig-align="center"}

## Generation: Cite or Refuse {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/generation/step-04.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# Agentic RAG: The Tool-Use Loop {.center}

::: lead
Every concept so far runs retrieval exactly once per question. An agent gets to
check its own result first, and search again if it wasn't good enough.
:::

::: notes
The last mechanical concept before evaluation. Retrieval is unchanged, the same
`.search()` as every earlier topic. What changes is the wrapper. It reads the score,
then pays for a second call only when the first one earns it.
:::

---

## One search, or two?

The retrieval loop so far always asks once. An agent gets to check its own work first.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="90" width="380" height="220"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="250" y="140" text-anchor="middle">One-shot retrieval</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="185" text-anchor="middle">search once</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="215" text-anchor="middle">answer immediately</text>
    <text class="note" x="250" y="245" text-anchor="middle">no matter how weak the hit</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card hot" x="560" y="90" width="380" height="220"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="750" y="140" text-anchor="middle">Agentic retrieval</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="185" text-anchor="middle">search, then check the score</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="215" text-anchor="middle">reformulate if it's weak</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="245" text-anchor="middle">search again before answering</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">The only difference: the ability to look at its own result and try again.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Every topic so far is one fixed pass: embed, retrieve, answer. Agentic RAG exposes that same search_corpus as a tool the model can call, inspect, and call again. Only the choice of when to stop is new.
:::

---

## The decision loop

One tool call, one confidence check, and a fork: answer now, or search again first.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node" x="40" y="140" width="170" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="125" y="192" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 210 185 L 278 185"/>
    <text class="note" x="245" y="165" text-anchor="middle">call</text>
    <rect class="node feat" x="280" y="140" width="230" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="395" y="192" text-anchor="middle">search_corpus</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 510 185 L 588 185"/>
    <text class="note" x="550" y="165" text-anchor="middle">check</text>
    <polygon class="node" points="680,110 770,185 680,260 590,185"/>
    <text class="cap" x="680" y="190" text-anchor="middle">confident?</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 770 185 L 828 185"/>
    <text class="note" x="800" y="165" text-anchor="middle">yes</text>
    <rect class="node" x="830" y="140" width="140" height="90"/>
    <text class="cap" x="900" y="192" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 680 260 C 680 335, 395 335, 395 232"/>
    <text class="note" x="540" y="355" text-anchor="middle">no -> reformulate, search_corpus again</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
run_agent() runs a scripted loop. It calls search_corpus once and takes the loop-back edge only if the top score lands under 0.2. The retry is capped at one, with no second pass through the diamond, so it can never spiral into unbounded search.
:::

---

## A captured trace

The question: "what's up with the database when it just kind of goes down." Vague on purpose.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="50" width="880" height="110"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="95">search_corpus("what's up with the database ... goes down")</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="130">-> top: On-call Runbook: Database Outage v2, score 0.176</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="152" style="fill:var(--amber)">0.176 &lt; 0.2 -> not confident, reformulate</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="200" width="880" height="110"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="245">search_corpus("what's with database ... goes down")</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="280">-> top: On-call Runbook: Database Outage v2, score 0.176</text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="302">same top doc, same score, so the second call only confirmed the first</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">finish: "If the primary database is unreachable, page the database on-call immediately. [1]"</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The constant named DEMO_QUERY_WEAK ("what happens during a database outage") scores 0.212, just over the 0.2 bar, so it never fires a second call. The vaguer phrasing shown here is what pushes the first score under threshold. Both calls land on the same document, so the reformulation only had to clear the bar.
:::

---

## How the rewrite happens

The reformulation is a word filter. It drops every word four letters or shorter.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="70" width="880" height="120"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="140" xml:space="preserve"><tspan>what's </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">up </tspan><tspan>with </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">the </tspan><tspan>database when </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">it </tspan><tspan>just kind </tspan><tspan style="fill:var(--muted);text-decoration:line-through">of </tspan><tspan>goes down</tspan></text>
    <text class="note" x="90" y="175">grey + struck: words of length 3 or less</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 500 190 L 500 235"/>
    <rect class="card hot" x="60" y="245" width="880" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="90" y="298">what's with database when just kind goes down</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="370" text-anchor="middle">A production agent would rewrite with an LLM call. This toy loop stays deterministic, so the figure reproduces the same way every time.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The rule is `" ".join(w for w in question.split() if len(w) > 3)`. Word length only, no notion of meaning. Keeping words of four characters or more drops the filler and leaves the content words, which is why the cleaned query still points at the same runbook.
:::

---

## Not blind repetition

The agent only pays for a second search when the first one earns it.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="30" y="30" width="940" height="150"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="55" text-anchor="middle">CONFIDENT ON THE FIRST TRY, ONE CALL</text>
    <rect class="node" x="60" y="80" width="150" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="135" y="122" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 210 115 L 268 115"/>
    <rect class="node" x="270" y="80" width="220" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="380" y="122" text-anchor="middle">search_corpus</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 490 115 L 548 115"/>
    <rect class="node" x="550" y="80" width="150" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="625" y="122" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="850" y="122" text-anchor="middle">score 0.212</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="zone" x="30" y="210" width="940" height="170"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="235" text-anchor="middle">WEAK ON THE FIRST TRY, TWO CALLS</text>
    <rect class="node" x="60" y="260" width="130" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="125" y="302" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 190 295 L 238 295"/>
    <rect class="node" x="240" y="260" width="190" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="335" y="302" text-anchor="middle">search_corpus</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 430 295 L 478 295"/>
    <rect class="node feat" x="480" y="260" width="190" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="575" y="302" text-anchor="middle">search again</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 670 295 L 718 295"/>
    <rect class="node" x="720" y="260" width="130" height="70"/>
    <text class="cap" x="785" y="302" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="920" y="302" text-anchor="middle">0.176</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Same corpus, same k, same code path. Only the confidence of the first hit changes the trace. Searching twice on every question would double latency and cost with no gain on the ones already nailed. The threshold keeps the expensive path for when it pays off.
:::

---

## Scoring a multi-hop question

Single-hop scoring asks whether one good doc surfaced. Multi-hop scoring asks whether every hop's doc did.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node" x="30" y="60" width="150" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="105" y="106" text-anchor="middle">question</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 180 100 L 223 100"/>
    <rect class="node feat" x="225" y="60" width="180" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="315" y="106" text-anchor="middle">search 1</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 405 100 L 448 100"/>
    <rect class="node feat" x="450" y="60" width="180" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="540" y="106" text-anchor="middle">search 2</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 630 100 L 673 100"/>
    <rect class="node" x="675" y="60" width="160" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="755" y="106" text-anchor="middle">answer</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 315 140 L 315 197"/>
    <rect class="card" x="225" y="200" width="180" height="66"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="248" y="240">needs doc A</text>
    <path d="M 355 236 L 366 248 L 388 224" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 540 140 L 540 197"/>
    <rect class="card" x="450" y="200" width="180" height="66"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="473" y="240">needs doc B</text>
    <path d="M 580 236 L 591 248 L 613 224" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 388 224 C 480 150, 660 150, 742 166"/>
    <path class="flow draw fragment" pathLength="1" d="M 613 224 C 665 185, 705 178, 758 166"/>
    <path d="M 736 168 L 750 184 L 784 150" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="6" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
    <text class="note" x="760" y="208" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">all evidence in</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle">Single-hop: was one relevant doc anywhere in the top-K?</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">Multi-hop: was doc A AND doc B both retrieved across the chain? Miss one and the final answer is wrong.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Top-K recall normally counts a question solved if any gold doc appears in the results. A multi-hop question splits its answer across two documents, so retrieving one and missing the other looks like a partial win but still gives a wrong answer. The fix is to score the whole chain, requiring every supporting doc across all the searches.
:::


# Agentic RAG: The Tool-Use Loop

## Agentic RAG: The Tool-Use Loop {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/agentic-rag/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Agentic RAG: The Tool-Use Loop {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/agentic-rag/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}

---

# Evaluation: How You Know It's Working {.center}

::: lead
A demo can look convincing and still be wrong. The only way to trust a RAG
system is to run it against questions with known answers, and grade it.
:::

Every concept so far is a design choice: chunking, retrieval, hybrid search,
reranking, generation. Evaluation is how you find out whether those choices
work.

::: notes
Nothing built so far earns trust just because a one-off demo looks
reasonable. Evaluation is a fixed question set with a known answer for each,
rerun automatically on every pipeline change, so a regression shows up the
moment it appears.
:::

---

## A frozen set of questions, each with a known answer

Write down questions a user would ask, then name the one document that
answers each. Freeze the list. Score every future change against it,
unchanged.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="30" width="560" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="67">"parental leave weeks"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 60 L 700 60"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="30" width="250" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="67" style="font-size:13px">hr-06 &middot; Parental Leave Policy</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="110" width="560" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="147">"code review pull request size"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 140 L 700 140"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="110" width="250" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="147" style="font-size:13px">eng-08 &middot; Code Review Guidelines</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="190" width="560" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="227">"aurora vault encryption key rotation"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 220 L 700 220"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="190" width="250" height="60"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="227" style="font-size:13px">prod-08 &middot; Aurora Vault Encryption</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="290" width="560" height="60" style="stroke:var(--muted); stroke-dasharray:6 5"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="66" y="327">"what is the meaning of life"</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 610 320 L 700 320" style="stroke-dasharray:6 5"/>
    <rect class="card" x="710" y="290" width="250" height="60" style="stroke:var(--muted); stroke-dasharray:6 5"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="315" style="font-size:12px">no matching document</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="734" y="333" style="font-size:12px">in the corpus</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Three of the four have an answer in the corpus. The fourth has none. It
earns its place by testing what the system does when there is nothing to
find.
:::

---

## Recall@k is a looser bar than faithful

Recall@k asks whether the right document is anywhere in the top few
results. Faithful asks a stricter question. Is it the very top hit, with a
similarity score worth trusting?

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="zone" cx="280" cy="200" r="150"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="280" cy="200" r="100"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="280" cy="200" r="50"/>
    <circle cx="280" cy="140" r="9" fill="var(--accent)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="280" y="370">recall@5</text>
    <text class="note" x="280" y="392" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">right doc lands anywhere in the top 5</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="zone" cx="720" cy="200" r="150"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="720" cy="200" r="100"/>
    <circle class="zone" cx="720" cy="200" r="50"/>
    <circle cx="720" cy="200" r="9" fill="var(--feat)"/>
    <text class="cap" x="720" y="370">faithful</text>
    <text class="note" x="720" y="392" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">right doc is the #1 hit, above threshold</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A pipeline can pass recall@k and still fail at the top. The right document
sits in the list, but a weaker one outranks it -- and that is what gets
cited. Faithful is the stricter bar.
:::

---

## Running the eval

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="20" width="620" height="56"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="55">"parental leave weeks"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="48" r="24" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,48 L 817,58 L 834,36" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="100" width="620" height="56"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="135">"code review pull request size"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="128" r="24" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,128 L 817,138 L 834,116" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="180" width="620" height="56"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="215">"aurora vault encryption key rotation"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="208" r="24" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,208 L 817,218 L 834,196" fill="none" stroke="var(--feat)" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="260" width="620" height="56" style="stroke:#e06b9c"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="86" y="295">"what is the meaning of life"</text>
    <circle cx="820" cy="288" r="24" fill="none" stroke="#e06b9c" stroke-width="4"/>
    <path d="M 808,276 L 832,300 M 832,276 L 808,300" stroke="#e06b9c" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="360" text-anchor="middle">The one X is not a defect. It is the system correctly saying it does not know.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The toy pipeline ran against the four frozen questions. Three pass recall
and faithful. The fourth fails both, because nothing in the corpus covers
the meaning of life, and saying so beats guessing from the nearest chunk.
:::

---

## Back to where this deck started

A plain language model would have answered the meaning-of-life question
anyway, fluently and with no warning that it was guessing. This system
checks itself against the evidence first. It only speaks when it has some.

---

## Three lenses, one system

Retrieval quality, answer quality, and end-to-end usefulness are three
different questions. A system can pass one and fail the next.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="80" r="44"/>
    <text class="cap" x="150" y="86">retrieval</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="150" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">right docs found?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 196 84 L 636 182"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="200" r="44"/>
    <text class="cap" x="150" y="206">answer</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="270" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">correct + grounded?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 196 200 L 636 200"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <circle class="node" cx="150" cy="320" r="44"/>
    <text class="cap" x="150" y="326">utility</text>
    <text class="note" x="150" y="390" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">did it help?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 196 316 L 636 218"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="640" y="140" width="300" height="120"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="790" y="196">one number</text>
    <text class="note" x="790" y="230" text-anchor="middle">hides which lens broke</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
A system can ace retrieval and still write a weak answer, or nail the answer
and still lose the user on latency or tone. One combined score gives you a
grade but never the reason.
:::

---

## When an answer is wrong, where did it break?

A wrong answer breaks at one stage. Walk the tree until a branch says no.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="250" y="15" width="340" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="420" y="47" style="font-size:15px">evidence retrieved at all?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 592 41 L 676 41"/>
    <text class="note" x="634" y="34" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">no</text>
    <rect class="card hot" x="680" y="15" width="280" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="820" y="47" style="font-size:15px">corpus / chunking / embedding</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 420 69 L 420 103"/>
    <text class="note" x="432" y="92" style="font-size:12px">yes</text>
    <rect class="card" x="250" y="105" width="340" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="420" y="137" style="font-size:15px">ranked high enough to reach it?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 592 131 L 676 131"/>
    <text class="note" x="634" y="124" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">no</text>
    <rect class="card hot" x="680" y="105" width="280" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="820" y="137" style="font-size:15px">retrieval / reranking</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 420 159 L 420 193"/>
    <text class="note" x="432" y="182" style="font-size:12px">yes</text>
    <rect class="card" x="250" y="195" width="340" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="420" y="227" style="font-size:15px">did the model use it?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 592 221 L 676 221"/>
    <text class="note" x="634" y="214" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">no</text>
    <rect class="card hot" x="680" y="195" width="280" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="820" y="227" style="font-size:15px">generation</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <path class="edge" d="M 420 249 L 420 283"/>
    <text class="note" x="432" y="272" style="font-size:12px">yes</text>
    <rect class="card" x="250" y="285" width="340" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="420" y="317" style="font-size:15px">correct, but user unhappy?</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 592 311 L 676 311"/>
    <text class="note" x="634" y="304" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">yes</text>
    <rect class="card hot" x="680" y="285" width="280" height="52"/>
    <text class="cap" x="820" y="317" style="font-size:15px">product / presentation</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Fix the earliest failing stage first -- tuning the generator cannot help
when the evidence never arrived. Most "the model is dumb" complaints are a
retrieval or chunking fault one stage upstream.
:::

---

## The diagnosis picks the fix

The stage that failed tells you which lever to pull.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="40" width="420" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="250" y="86" style="font-size:15px">evidence missing or mis-ranked</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 466 80 L 534 80"/>
    <rect class="node" x="540" y="40" width="420" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="750" y="74">improve retrieval</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="100" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">corpus &middot; chunking &middot; reranker</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="150" width="420" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="250" y="196" style="font-size:15px">evidence there, answer wrong</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 466 190 L 534 190"/>
    <rect class="node" x="540" y="150" width="420" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="750" y="184">improve the generator</text>
    <text class="note" x="750" y="210" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">prompt &middot; context &middot; format</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="node ghost" x="40" y="285" width="920" height="80"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="320">fine-tuning is the last lever</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="348" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">used only after the same failure repeats</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Reaching for fine-tuning first is the common trap. It is slow, it is costly,
and it buries the retrieval bug the earlier stages would have surfaced for
free.
:::

---

## The eval set is its own discipline

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="24" width="440" height="300"/>
    <text class="cap" x="260" y="62">cover the space</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="108" style="font-size:15px">across frequency, difficulty,</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="136" style="font-size:15px">domain, freshness, user segment</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="196" style="font-size:15px">question types, all four:</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="224" style="font-size:15px">answerable &middot; unanswerable</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="252" style="font-size:15px">ambiguous &middot; multi-hop</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="520" y="24" width="440" height="300"/>
    <text class="cap" x="740" y="62">trust the labels</text>
    <text class="note" x="550" y="108" style="font-size:15px">human relevance judgments,</text>
    <text class="note" x="550" y="136" style="font-size:15px">or controlled synthetic questions</text>
    <text class="note" x="550" y="196" style="font-size:15px">passage-level labels beat</text>
    <text class="note" x="550" y="224" style="font-size:15px">document-level for debugging</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="372" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">in this deck, 2 of 4 eval queries first matched the wrong twin of a near-duplicate doc</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The two flagged queries were labeled against the stale twin of a duplicated
policy, so a correct retrieval scored as a miss. A four-question set still
hid a labeling bug. Ground truth is the hard part, harder than picking the
metric.
:::

---

## Past the toy pipeline

Four offline questions is where this deck stops. Production starts here.

```{=html}
<svg class="ragfig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="40" width="280" height="300"/>
    <text class="cap" x="180" y="88">stress-test</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="150" style="font-size:14px">distractors, contradictions</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="188" style="font-size:14px">stale + duplicate docs</text>
    <text class="note" x="70" y="226" style="font-size:14px">follow evidence or prior?</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="360" y="40" width="280" height="300"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="88">audit the judge</text>
    <text class="note" x="390" y="150" style="font-size:14px">validate vs human labels</text>
    <text class="note" x="390" y="188" style="font-size:14px">measure agreement</text>
    <text class="note" x="390" y="226" style="font-size:14px">test prompt, model, order</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment">
    <rect class="card" x="680" y="40" width="280" height="300"/>
    <text class="cap" x="820" y="88">go online</text>
    <text class="note" x="710" y="150" style="font-size:14px">resolution + follow-up rate</text>
    <text class="note" x="710" y="188" style="font-size:14px">task completion</text>
    <text class="note" x="710" y="226" style="font-size:14px">latency + cost as guardrails</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Each column is its own project. An LLM judge that never gets checked against
people drifts, confidently. The online numbers are the ones that pay for the
system, and they arrive last.
:::

---


# Evaluation: How You Know It Works

## Evaluation: How You Know It Works {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/evaluation/step-01.png){fig-align="center"}

## Evaluation: How You Know It Works {auto-animate=true}

![](assets/evaluation/step-02.png){fig-align="center"}
